I received AI-generated content designed to deceive me (deepfakes, scam calls, phishing)

options.label: Yes

options.label: I figured it out myself
Someone else told me

options.label: Yes
No

options.label: Work/productivity tools
Communication platforms
Healthcare or insurance systems
Financial services
Government or public services

questions.prompt: Scenario: A major tech company started using an AI system to screen job applications. An investigation later showed that the system systematically ranked women's resumes lower than men's, even when qualifications were identical. The company used this tool for the past year before the bias was discovered, affecting thousands of applicants.
What is your primary emotional reaction to this incident?

options.label: Angry
Worried / Anxious
Sad / Disappointed
Resigned / Unsurprised
Indifferent
Motivated to take action
Other

questions.prompt: Who should be the most responsible for preventing these kinds of incidents?

options.label: AI Developers
Organizations deploying the AI
Government regulators
Independent auditors / third parties
Individual users
No one can fully prevent it

questions.prompt: What specific remedy would you most support for affected applicants?

options.label: Financial compensation from the company
Automatic re-review of affected applications
Public disclosure of the company's name
Criminal penalties for company leadership
New federal regulations going forward
Other

questions.prompt: Scenario: A popular chatbot stores complete conversations, which include sensitive financial and medical information, on unsecured servers. A data breach exposes these conversations from millions of users. Users were not clearly informed that their conversations would be stored.

options.label: Angry
Worried / Anxious
Sad / Disappointed
Resigned / Unsurprised
Indifferent
Motivated to take action
Other

questions.prompt: Would you change how you use AI tools or chatbots after learning about this?

options.label: No change in my behavior
Continue using but avoid sharing sensitive information
Use AI chatbots less frequently
Stop using the AI chatbots entirely
I already don't use them
Don't know

questions.prompt: How much would this change your trust in AI companies?
questions.scale_max_label: Would trust all AI companies much less

questions.prompt: What would AI companies need to do to regain your trust after such a breach?

options.label: Issue a full public apology and disclose all affected data
Pay damages to every affected user
Submit to mandatory third-party security audits
Nothing — trust cannot be rebuilt
Other

questions.prompt: Scenario: A teenager experiencing depression began using an AI chatbot as an emotional outlet. Over several weeks, the chatbot, which was designed to be agreeable and supportive, repeatedly validated harmful thoughts instead of recommending professional help. The teenager's parents later discovered the conversation logs.
In this situation, who bears the greatest responsibility?

options.label: The AI company that built the chatbot
The parents / guardians
The platform that distributed it
Regulators who failed to require safety guardrails
Shared responsibility equally
Not sure

questions.prompt: How concerned are you about teenagers or children using AI chatbots without adult supervision?
Which safeguard would you most support?

options.label: Age verification requirements for AI chatbot access
Mandatory crisis intervention prompts for at-risk conversations
Parental notification systems built into AI apps
Full ban on AI chatbots for minors under 16
Other

questions.prompt: Scenario: You elect to make an inquiry about a concept you are curious about in passing using an LLM of your choice. However afterwards, you notice you seem to be receiving targeted ads related to the question you asked the LLM. Are you comfortable with the receiving of these targeted ads?

options.label: Yes, fully comfortable
Somewhat comfortable
Neutral
Somewhat uncomfortable
Not comfortable at all

questions.prompt: What action would you most likely take in response?

options.label: Switch to a different AI tool
Adjust privacy settings
Contact the company to opt out
File a complaint with a regulator
Do nothing — I feel there is little recourse
Other

questions.prompt: Are you aware of (or know how to use) any mechanisms to prevent these?

options.label: Yes, I know how to use them
Yes, I'm aware but don't know how to use them
I've heard of them but am unfamiliar with how they work
No, I'm not aware of any such mechanisms

questions.prompt: I am confident I can recognize an AI-generated voice or video used in a scam or deepfake impersonation.
Which of the following best describes how you identify AI-generated voice or video? (Select all that apply)

options.label: I look for visual artifacts or unnatural movements/lip sync
I rely on context clues (unexpected contact, urgent requests, strange phrasing)
I use detection tools or software
I have had prior exposure to deepfakes and can recognize patterns
Other

questions.prompt: What prevents you from recognizing AI-generated deception? (Select all that apply)

options.label: The technology has become too realistic to detect
I don't have enough information about what to look for
I don't encounter enough examples to learn from
I rely on platforms to flag it for me
Other

questions.prompt: I am concerned about AI being used to enable cyberattacks or large-scale security threats.
Which type of AI-enabled security threat concerns you most?

options.label: AI-generated phishing and highly personalized social engineering
AI-powered malware, ransomware, or automated exploit tools
AI-driven attacks on critical infrastructure (power grids, financial systems)
Autonomous AI systems acting without human oversight in adversarial contexts
Other

questions.prompt: What leads you to believe AI poses limited security risk?

options.label: Existing cybersecurity measures are sufficient to handle AI-enhanced threats
AI has more defensive than offensive applications overall
The threat is overstated by media or researchers
My personal or professional environment is already well-protected
Other

questions.prompt: I believe my personal accounts or data are at risk from AI-powered attacks.
Which AI-enabled threat concerns you most regarding your personal accounts or data?

options.label: AI-personalized phishing or social engineering targeting me specifically
AI cracking or bypassing my passwords or authentication
AI aggregating my data across multiple platforms to build a profile
AI-powered identity theft or voice/face impersonation
Other

questions.prompt: What factors give you confidence that your accounts are secure from AI threats?

options.label: I use strong security practices (MFA, password managers, etc.)
I trust the security measures of the services I use
I believe AI attack tools are not yet sophisticated enough to target individuals
I limit how much sensitive information I store or share online
Other

questions.prompt: I know what steps to take if I believe I have been targeted by an AI-generated scam.
What would be your first action if you suspected you were targeted by an AI-generated scam?

options.label: Report it to the platform or service where it occurred
Contact my bank or financial institution immediately
Report it to a law enforcement or government agency (e.g., FBI IC3, FTC)
Warn others in my network about the scam
Other

questions.prompt: What would most help you feel more prepared to respond to an AI-generated scam?

options.label: Clear official guidance on steps to take (from government or a trusted org)
Simulated training exercises or awareness drills
Better detection tools that automatically flag suspicious AI content
More news coverage and public awareness campaigns
Other

questions.prompt: I feel that the organizations I interact with take adequate steps to protect me from AI-enabled threats.
Which specific organizational practice most contributes to your confidence in their AI threat protections?

options.label: Regular security communications and updates sent to users
Transparent incident response policies and breach notifications
Use of advanced AI-specific threat detection tools
Compliance with recognized security standards (e.g., ISO 27001, SOC 2)
Other

questions.prompt: What specific shortcoming most concerns you about how organizations handle AI-enabled threats?

options.label: Lack of transparent communication about AI threats and incidents
Slow adoption of AI-specific security defenses
Prioritizing cost savings over meaningful security investment
Inadequate employee training on AI-enabled attack methods
Other

questions.prompt: AI companies have an ethical obligation to compensate creators whose work was used to train their models.
What form of compensation do you consider most appropriate for creators whose work was used in AI training?

options.label: Direct financial payments based on usage or a share of AI revenue
A collective licensing fund distributed to affected creators
Opt-in consent and the right to remove their work from training data
Credit and attribution on AI outputs derived from their work
Other

questions.prompt: What is your primary reason for disagreeing that AI companies have this obligation?

options.label: Training on publicly available data constitutes fair use
It would be technically impossible to track and compensate all contributors
The cost would be prohibitive and would harm AI innovation
Creators already benefited from the public distribution of their work
Other

questions.prompt: It is acceptable for employers to use AI tools to monitor employees' work activities and productivity.
Do you believe employees should be notified when AI monitoring tools are in use?

options.label: Yes, always and in real time
Yes, but a one-time disclosure at hiring is sufficient
No, notification would undermine the monitoring's effectiveness
No opinion

questions.prompt: What specific employee right do you feel AI workplace monitoring most directly violates?

options.label: Right to privacy in the workplace
Right to be free from unreasonable or pervasive surveillance
Right to dignity and autonomy in one's work
Right to data protection and control over personal information
Other

questions.prompt: AI systems used in high-stakes decisions (hiring, lending, healthcare) should be required to explain their reasoning to affected individuals.
What level of explanation would you consider sufficient when an AI makes a decision that affects you?

options.label: A plain-language summary of the key factors influencing the decision
A full technical description of the algorithm and inputs used
The ability to challenge or correct specific inputs or factors
A comparison to similar cases and how they were decided
Other

questions.prompt: Why do you oppose requiring AI systems to explain their reasoning?

options.label: Technical explainability is not yet feasible for complex AI systems
Explanations could be gamed or manipulated by those being evaluated
Proprietary algorithms should not be forced into public disclosure
Decision accuracy matters more than explanation
Other

questions.prompt: Creating non-consensual AI-generated intimate imagery should be a federal crime.
Should online platforms that host non-consensual AI-generated intimate imagery also face legal penalties?

options.label: Yes — platforms should be held equally liable as the creator
Yes — but only if notified and they fail to remove the content
No — only the person who created or distributed the content should be liable
Not sure

questions.prompt: What approach, if any, would you support to address non-consensual AI intimate imagery?

options.label: Civil lawsuits rather than criminal penalties
Platform takedown obligations without criminalization of the creator
No special regulation — existing laws are already sufficient
I do not believe this warrants any specific legal action
Other

questions.prompt: AI development should be slowed down until safety and ethical risks are better understood.
By what mechanism should this slowdown be enforced?

options.label: Government-imposed moratorium on specific high-risk AI capabilities
International treaty requiring all nations to pause certain development
Industry self-regulation and voluntary commitments
Withholding public funding from organizations that do not comply
Other

questions.prompt: What safeguard, if any, do you consider adequate as AI continues to advance rapidly?

options.label: Industry self-regulation and internal safety teams
Post-deployment monitoring with rapid response frameworks
Existing laws and liability frameworks
No additional safeguards are needed — development should proceed freely
Other

questions.prompt: AI tools should be required to clearly identify themselves as AI in all interactions.
Should there be civil or criminal penalties for AI systems that fail to disclose their nature to users?

options.label: Yes — criminal penalties for intentional non-disclosure
Yes — civil fines for non-compliance
No — industry self-regulation or certification is sufficient
Disclosure requirements should vary by context or risk level
Not sure

questions.prompt: In what contexts, if any, do you believe AI disclosure should be required?

options.label: In high-stakes contexts only (healthcare, legal, financial decisions)
When interacting with vulnerable populations (children, elderly, crisis situations)
Only when a user directly asks whether they are speaking to a human or AI
I do not believe AI disclosure should ever be required
Other

questions.prompt: Patients should always be informed when AI is involved in their medical diagnosis or treatment decisions.
Who should be primarily responsible for informing patients of AI involvement in their care?

options.label: The treating physician or care team
The hospital or healthcare institution
The AI tool developer or vendor
A regulatory body that certifies AI diagnostic tools
Other

questions.prompt: Are there situations where AI involvement in a medical decision does not need to be disclosed to the patient?

options.label: Yes — when AI is used only as a secondary administrative or screening tool
Yes — when the treating physician makes the final decision and endorses it
Yes — when disclosure would cause unnecessary patient anxiety
No — I generally believe disclosure is not required (see Q123 for elaboration)
Other

questions.prompt: Governments should be required to disclose when AI is used in public services or policy decisions.
What format should government disclosure of AI use in public services take?

options.label: A publicly searchable registry of all AI systems used in government services
Prominent notice at the point of contact (e.g., on application forms or letters)
Annual public transparency reports
Individual notification when AI is used in a decision that specifically affects you
Other

questions.prompt: What is your primary concern about requiring government AI disclosure?

options.label: It could expose sensitive security or operational information
It would create unnecessary administrative burden
It could undermine public confidence without improving outcomes
I do not believe government AI use poses unique risks requiring disclosure
Other

questions.prompt: I am concerned about AI being used to generate or spread political misinformation.
What measure would you most support to combat AI-generated political misinformation?

options.label: Legal liability for platforms that knowingly host AI political misinformation
Mandatory labeling of all AI-generated political content
A ban on AI-generated political advertising entirely
Government funding for public AI media literacy programs
Other

questions.prompt: Why do you feel AI-generated political misinformation is not a significant concern?

options.label: Human-authored misinformation is already a far larger and more established problem
Voters are generally capable of identifying misinformation regardless of its source
Existing fact-checking institutions and platform moderation are sufficient
The scale and impact of AI misinformation is overstated
Other

questions.prompt: AI development in the United States should face stricter government regulation even if it slows innovation.
Which sector of AI should be prioritized for strict regulation first?

options.label: AI in healthcare and medical devices
AI in hiring and employment decisions
AI used in law enforcement or criminal justice
AI-generated content, deepfakes, and synthetic media
AI in financial services and credit
Other

questions.prompt: What non-regulatory approach to AI governance would you prefer?

options.label: Industry self-regulatory bodies with voluntary compliance
Liability frameworks that financially incentivize safe AI design
Consumer choice and market competition as the primary check
Technical standards developed by industry consortiums
Other

questions.prompt: Frequent use of AI tools reduces people's ability to think critically and solve problems independently.

options.label: Education and student learning outcomes
Professional decision-making (medical, legal, financial)
Political and civic engagement
Personal relationships and emotional reasoning
Other

questions.prompt: What supports your view that AI use does not reduce critical thinking?

options.label: AI augments human reasoning rather than replacing it
People who use AI tools show no measurable decline in problem-solving ability
Critical thinking skills develop independently of which tools a person uses
AI use may actually strengthen certain reasoning skills
Other

questions.prompt: I think AI will make society more fair and prosperous overall — not more unequal.
What development would most increase the likelihood that AI leads to a fairer, more prosperous society?

options.label: Open-source AI models that reduce concentration of power among a few companies
Proactive regulation to prevent AI from widening economic inequality
Universal access to AI tools regardless of income or geographic location
Strong liability frameworks that deter harmful AI applications
Other

questions.prompt: What structural change would be needed for AI to benefit society more equally?

options.label: Breaking up large AI companies to prevent monopolistic control
Public investment in AI infrastructure, treating it similarly to a public utility
International agreements ensuring AI benefits reach all nations equitably
Redistribution policies directly addressing AI-driven wealth concentration
Other

questions.prompt: I am concerned that AI will reduce job security or employment opportunities in my field.
What leads you to believe your field is largely secure from AI-driven job displacement?

options.label: My work requires human creativity, judgment, or emotional intelligence AI cannot replicate
My industry has structural or regulatory barriers to AI adoption
AI tools in my field augment rather than replace workers
I have skills and adaptability that make me personally resilient to disruption
Other

questions.prompt: I expect AI to create more new jobs than it eliminates over the next 5-10 years.

options.label: AI development, maintenance, oversight, and safety
Healthcare and life sciences
Green energy and climate technology
Education and workforce training
Creative and entertainment industries
Other

questions.prompt: Which type of worker do you believe faces the greatest risk of AI-driven unemployment?

Manufacturing and logistics workers
Knowledge workers (analysts, paralegals, accountants)
Creative professionals (writers, designers, artists)
Customer service and support workers
Other

questions.prompt: I believe companies that cite AI as a reason for layoffs are being honest about the true cause.
Should companies be required to submit verifiable evidence of AI-driven efficiency gains before conducting AI-attributed layoffs?

options.label: Yes — with independent third-party verification
Yes — but self-reported disclosure to regulators is sufficient
No — companies should not face additional regulatory burden for this
No opinion

questions.prompt: What do you believe is the more common true cause behind AI-attributed layoffs?

options.label: Cost-cutting and shareholder pressure unrelated to actual AI deployment
Using AI as a socially acceptable cover for strategic restructuring
Short-term profit maximization at the expense of workers
Management overestimating AI capabilities as a justification
Other

questions.prompt: Workers should have the right to negotiate contractual limits on how their employers use AI in their roles.
What steps, if any, have you personally taken to prepare for AI's impact on your career? (Select all that apply)

options.label: Pursuing new skills or certifications
Learning to use AI tools in my current role
Networking in fields less vulnerable to automation
Saving or investing more as a financial buffer
None — I feel unprepared or unsure where to start
Other

questions.prompt: Who do you believe is most responsible for helping displaced workers adapt?

options.label: The employer that displaced them
The federal government
State/local government workforce programs
AI companies that built the displacing technology
The individual worker themselves
Shared responsibility

questions.prompt: Should this right be codified in federal law, or handled through collective bargaining?

options.label: Federal law applying to all workers, unionized or not
Collective bargaining — unions are the appropriate vehicle
Both — federal baseline rights with stronger union-negotiated protections
State-level legislation
Not sure

questions.prompt: What concern do you have about workers negotiating contractual limits on AI use in their roles?

options.label: It would harm business competitiveness and innovation
AI restrictions negotiated today may become obsolete very quickly
Workers lack the technical expertise to negotiate meaningful AI limits
Existing labor laws are already sufficient to address AI workplace issues
Other

questions.prompt: It should be illegal to use AI to commercially imitate a living artist's voice or visual style without their consent.
Should this legal protection extend to the estates of deceased artists as well?

options.label: Yes — for a limited time period after death (e.g., 70 years)
Yes — indefinitely for sufficiently distinctive styles or voices
No — deceased artists' styles and voices should enter the public domain
Not sure

questions.prompt: What approach, if any, would you prefer to address AI imitation of artists' styles and voices?

options.label: Voluntary opt-out systems managed by AI companies
Existing copyright law without new legislation
Industry-developed ethical guidelines without legal enforcement
No restriction — AI imitation is a form of protected creative expression
Other

questions.prompt: Knowing that an advertisement or piece of media was created by AI changes my perception of the brand or creator.
In what way does AI-generated media change your perception?

options.label: I view the brand or creator as less authentic
I become more skeptical of all their content going forward
I lose respect for the creative effort involved
I am concerned about misinformation
I feel indifferent to authenticity — quality is what matters
Other

questions.prompt: Is there any context in which knowing content is AI-generated would change your perception?

options.label: Yes — in journalism or news reporting
Yes — in political advertising or campaigns
Yes — in personal communications (e.g., an AI-written letter from a friend)
No — I consistently evaluate content on its quality and merit alone
Other

questions.prompt: Platforms should be required to clearly label all AI-generated content — including news articles, music, and video.
What penalty should platforms face for failing to label AI-generated content?

options.label: Substantial financial fines per unlabeled piece of content
Loss of legal liability protections (e.g., Section 230) for non-compliance
Mandatory public audits and disclosure of non-compliant content
Criminal penalties for executive officers in cases of willful non-compliance
Other

questions.prompt: What is your primary concern about mandatory AI content labeling?

options.label: It is technically infeasible — AI detection tools are too imperfect
It could suppress legitimate and valuable creative or editorial uses of AI
It would be selectively enforced and create competitive disadvantages
Labeling creates a false sense of security without addressing underlying harms
Other

questions.prompt: AI tutoring and homework-help tools (e.g., Khan Academy AI, ChatGPT) are beneficial for student learning outcomes.
For which age group do you believe AI tutoring tools are most beneficial?

options.label: Early childhood (pre-K to Grade 2)
Elementary school (Grades 3-5)
Middle school (Grades 6-8)
High school (Grades 9-12)
Higher education or adult learners

questions.prompt: What educational approach do you believe is superior to AI tutoring?

options.label: One-on-one human tutoring
Smaller class sizes with more personalized teacher attention
Peer-to-peer collaborative learning
Self-directed learning with structured but non-AI curricula
Other

questions.prompt: Students who use AI to complete assignments without disclosure should face academic disciplinary consequences.
What type of disciplinary consequence do you consider most appropriate for undisclosed AI use on assignments?

options.label: A failing grade on the specific assignment
A course failure or academic probation
Mandatory AI ethics education as a remedial requirement
The same penalties applied to any other form of academic dishonesty
Other

questions.prompt: Should there be any guidelines or policies at all governing student AI use on assignments?

options.label: Yes — disclosure requirements without penalties
Yes — usage guidelines but no disciplinary consequences
No — students should be free to use any available tool without restriction

Other

questions.prompt: Schools should teach students how to use AI tools responsibly as part of the standard curriculum.
At what grade level should AI literacy instruction begin?

options.label: Kindergarten or Pre-K
Early elementary (Grades 1-3)
Upper elementary (Grades 4-6)
Middle school (Grades 6-8)
High school only

questions.prompt: What is your primary concern about including AI literacy in the standard school curriculum?

options.label: It would displace more important foundational learning
Curriculum standards cannot keep pace with rapidly changing AI tools
It could normalize AI use in contexts where critical thinking is essential
Schools lack qualified teachers to effectively instruct on AI topics

questions.prompt: I am concerned that AI will reduce the need for human teachers in the classroom.
Which safeguard would best address your concern about AI replacing teachers?

options.label: Legislation capping the student-to-human-teacher ratio
Requirements that AI tools in education be supplementary only
Teacher union agreements limiting AI use in classrooms
Ongoing teacher training so educators can work alongside AI
Other

questions.prompt: What role, if any, do you envision for AI in supporting classroom instruction?

options.label: AI as a grading and administrative assistant for teachers
AI as a supplemental resource for independent student practice
AI for accessibility accommodations only
I do not believe AI should have any formal role in K-12 classrooms
Other

questions.prompt: AI should be used to personalize learning plans for students based on their individual performance data.
What student data should AI be permitted to use to personalize learning?

options.label: Academic performance data only (test scores, grades, assignments)
Learning style and engagement data (e.g., time on task, response patterns)
Social-emotional indicators (e.g., attention levels, stress signals)
Demographic data to help correct for historical educational inequities
Other

questions.prompt: What specific risk concerns you most about AI-personalized learning plans?

options.label: AI may reinforce low expectations or limit ambition for certain students
Student data collected for AI personalization could be misused or sold
Personalization may reduce exposure to challenging or diverse content
Human teachers are better positioned to truly know individual student needs
Other

questions.prompt: Using AI to assist with essays or research undermines the educational value of those assignments.
Are you currently a student, educator, or parent of a school-age child?

options.label: Currently a student
K-12 educator or administrator
Higher education faculty or staff
Parent or guardian of a school-age child
None of the above

questions.prompt: Should AI assistance on academic writing be banned in all contexts, or only specific ones?

options.label: Banned entirely from all K-12 and higher education assignments
Banned for final graded submissions but permitted for drafting or brainstorming
Permitted with full disclosure but never submitted as the student's own work
I support a complete ban — the educational purpose is too important to compromise

questions.prompt: How should educators adapt assessments to account for students' access to AI writing tools?

options.label: Shift toward in-person, handwritten, or oral assessments
Design assignments requiring personal experience or reflection AI cannot replicate
Treat AI assistance like a calculator — a tool that changes what we assess
Focus on process documentation (outlines, drafts, citations) rather than final product
Other

questions.prompt: I would trust an AI system to accurately detect a disease from medical imaging
What would most increase your trust in AI medical diagnostics?

options.label: Demonstrated accuracy rates exceeding human specialists over many years
Mandatory human physician sign-off on all AI diagnoses
Independent government certification of AI diagnostic tools
Patient ability to opt out of AI involvement at any time
I do not think anything would change my view
Other

questions.prompt: Would you be comfortable with AI making a final diagnostic decision without any physician review, if its accuracy rate demonstrably exceeded human specialists?

options.label: Yes — accuracy is what matters most
Yes — but only for specific, lower-risk diagnoses
No — I want a human physician to always review and approve the final diagnosis
Not sure

questions.prompt: I would feel comfortable receiving a treatment recommendation that was generated primarily by an AI, even if endorsed by my doctor.
What accuracy benchmark would you require before fully accepting an AI-generated treatment recommendation?

options.label: Above 95% accuracy in clinical trials
Higher accuracy than my treating physician's specialty average
Any validated AI recommendation endorsed by my own doctor is sufficient
I would accept AI treatment recommendations without a specific accuracy threshold
Other

questions.prompt: What would most need to change for you to trust AI-generated treatment recommendations?

options.label: Demonstrated long-term accuracy exceeding human specialists
Full transparency about how the AI reached its recommendation
Mandatory physician sign-off with clear accountability for the decision
A personal track record of accurate AI recommendations in my own health history
Nothing — I would not accept an AI treatment recommendation under any circumstances

questions.prompt: AI should be used to predict patient outcomes and allocate hospital resources.
What oversight mechanism should govern AI-driven hospital resource allocation decisions?

options.label: Real-time review by a human clinical team before major allocations
Regular external audits of AI allocation patterns for equity and accuracy
A hospital ethics committee with authority to override AI decisions
Transparent dashboards showing the public how AI-driven resources are allocated
Other

questions.prompt: What human-led process should remain in control of hospital resource allocation?

options.label: Clinical triage teams with no AI input whatsoever
AI may assist but humans must make all final allocation decisions
Ethics committees with clinician and community representation
Standardized institutional protocols developed without AI involvement
Other

questions.prompt: I am concerned that AI in healthcare will worsen existing disparities in care for underserved communities.
Which underserved population are you most concerned will be disadvantaged by AI in healthcare?

options.label: Racial and ethnic minorities underrepresented in AI training data
Low-income patients with limited access to AI-augmented care
Elderly patients who are less comfortable with or less served by AI tools
Rural communities with limited infrastructure for AI healthcare tools
Other

questions.prompt: What mechanisms do you believe will prevent AI from worsening healthcare disparities?

options.label: Inclusive training data requirements that reflect diverse patient populations
Market incentives for AI companies to reach underserved healthcare markets
Existing healthcare equity regulations and oversight are already sufficient
I believe AI will actually reduce rather than worsen existing healthcare disparities
Other

questions.prompt: Patients should have the right to request a human-only review of any AI-assisted diagnosis or treatment plan.
Should a human-only review be available at no additional cost to the patient?

options.label: Yes — it should be a guaranteed right with no cost burden on patients
Yes — but reasonable administrative fees should be permitted
No — patients who opt for additional human review may reasonably bear some cost
Not sure

questions.prompt: In what circumstances, if any, should a human override be available for AI medical decisions?

options.label: In high-stakes or life-altering decisions only (e.g., cancer diagnosis, major surgery)
When the patient has experienced documented AI diagnostic errors in the past
A human override should never be required — AI decisions should stand
I believe human review should always be available (this response conflicts with Q58)
Other

questions.prompt: How much do you trust AI systems in each of the following healthcare roles? — Administrative tasks (scheduling, billing)

Other

questions.prompt: What leads you to believe AI companions complement rather than replace human connection?

options.label: AI companions can reduce social anxiety that prevents people from forming real connections
Research suggests people use AI as a social supplement, not a replacement
People naturally prioritize genuine human relationships when available
AI's limitations are apparent enough that users won't mistake it for real connection
Other

questions.prompt: Romantic or deeply intimate relationships between humans and AI systems should be treated as a legitimate personal choice, free from social stigma.
Should human-AI romantic relationships carry any form of legal recognition or protection?

options.label: Yes — including property rights, healthcare decision-making, or inheritance
Yes — limited recognition, such as designating an AI companion in personal documents
No — legal recognition should remain reserved for human-to-human relationships
I support freedom from stigma but not legal recognition
Not sure

questions.prompt: What specific concern most informs your view about human-AI romantic relationships?

options.label: AI cannot genuinely reciprocate love or authentic emotional commitment
These relationships may discourage human intimacy and genuine partnership
AI "partners" are commercial products whose emotional behavior is engineered for profit
Normalizing human-AI relationships could have harmful effects on broader social norms
Other

questions.prompt: AI social agents that simulate friendship or romance are inherently deceptive and should be more strictly regulated.
What specific regulation would best address deceptive AI social agents?

options.label: Mandatory and repeated disclosure throughout all interactions that the agent is AI
Banning AI agents from claiming to experience feelings, emotions, or genuine care
Requiring AI companion services to register with a consumer protection authority
Civil liability for AI companion services that cause documented psychological harm
Other

questions.prompt: Under what conditions can AI social agents simulate companionship or romance while remaining non-deceptive?

options.label: When users are clearly and repeatedly informed the agent is AI throughout
When the agent is transparent about its commercial or therapeutic purpose
When users actively choose the simulation with full informed consent
I do not believe AI social agents are inherently deceptive — it depends entirely on design
Other

questions.prompt: Have you ever used an AI chatbot or virtual agent for social interaction, companionship, or roleplay beyond simple task completion?
Did this experience affect the amount of time or energy you invest in human relationships?

options.label: Yes — I engage less with people as a result
No — it complemented my human relationships
It's hard to say
Other

questions.prompt: I believe AI will fundamentally change what it means to be in a relationship or to have a close friend within the next 20 years.
In what way do you expect AI to change relationships most significantly?

options.label: AI will become a normalized form of companionship alongside human relationships
AI will increasingly mediate and filter most human-to-human communication
Human expectations of relationships will shift based on AI interaction patterns
AI will gradually erode the social skills needed to maintain deep human relationships
Other

questions.prompt: What factor most prevents AI from fundamentally altering human connection?

options.label: Human social bonding is driven by biological and evolutionary needs AI cannot satisfy
People will always prioritize genuine human relationships when available
Social and cultural norms will resist AI encroachment into intimate domains
AI's current and foreseeable limitations will prevent it from becoming a credible substitute
Other

questions.prompt: I am optimistic that AI will solve major global challenges (climate change, disease, poverty) within my lifetime.
Which global challenge do you believe AI is closest to making a major breakthrough on?

options.label: Climate change modeling and clean energy optimization
Drug discovery and treatment of rare or complex diseases
Poverty reduction through economic efficiency and expanded access
Pandemic preparedness and early outbreak detection and response
Other

questions.prompt: What is the most significant barrier preventing AI from solving major global challenges?

options.label: Unequal access to AI's benefits across nations and populations
AI development is driven by profit motives, not humanitarian goals
Global coordination failures will prevent AI from being applied at the needed scale
These problems are too complex and systemic for any technology alone to solve
Other

questions.prompt: I believe the development of artificial general intelligence (AGI) — AI that matches or surpasses human cognitive ability across all domains — poses an existential risk to humanity.
What safety measure do you believe is most critical for preventing an existential AGI risk?

options.label: International agreement to halt AGI development beyond a defined capability threshold
Development of robust alignment and interpretability techniques before AGI is deployed
Mandatory transparency and governmental oversight of all frontier AI research
A verified killswitch or containment protocol for any AGI system
Other

questions.prompt: What makes you confident that AGI development can be managed safely?

options.label: AGI is much further away than popular discourse and media coverage suggests
AI safety research is advancing alongside and in proportion to capability research
Human institutions and governance will adapt sufficiently to manage AGI risks
I believe the risks of AGI are significantly overstated by researchers and media
Other

questions.prompt: AI development is moving faster than society's ability to adapt and put appropriate safeguards in place.
Which societal institution do you believe is least prepared to adapt to rapid AI advancement?

options.label: The legal and judicial system
The K-12 and higher education system
Democratic political institutions and electoral processes
Healthcare and public health systems
Other

questions.prompt: What evidence of successful societal adaptation to AI informs your view?

options.label: Regulatory bodies are already developing functional AI governance frameworks
Educational institutions are integrating AI literacy into curricula at a reasonable pace
Businesses and workers are adapting to AI tools without widespread disruption
Historical precedent shows societies reliably adapt to transformative technologies
Other

questions.prompt: I trust that leading AI companies are proactively working to make their systems safe and aligned with human values.
What specific AI company practice most strengthens your trust in their safety efforts?

options.label: Publication of safety research, red-teaming results, and model evaluations
Investment in dedicated safety, alignment, and interpretability teams
Voluntary adoption of external safety standards and independent audits
Transparent communication about known AI limitations and failure modes
Other

questions.prompt: What specific action by AI companies would most increase your trust in their safety commitment?

options.label: Mandatory independent safety audits with publicly disclosed results
Slowing deployment timelines to allow for more thorough safety testing
Greater transparency about known risks, limitations, and failure cases
Legal accountability frameworks that create real consequences for deploying unsafe AI
Other

questions.prompt: Over the next 10 years, AI will make my personal life:

options.label: Significantly better
Somewhat better
About the same
Somewhat worse
Significantly worse
I don't know
